coredata.dc:title: Artificial intelligence-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection of fluoride ion by Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emssion enhanced carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Liru
Zhang, Bianxiang
Zong, Zhiwei
Zhou, Wei
Shuang, Shaomin
Shi, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: Artificial intelligence (AI)-integrated smartphone-based handheld determination platform, based on 3D printed accessory, Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emssion enhanced carbon dots (CDs) test strip, and smartphone with self-developed YOLO v3 AI algorithm-based application, proves the feasibility for intelligent real-time on-site quantitation of F− through tracking a consecutive fluorescence (FL) colour change. CDs, manifesting dual emission of moderate green emission at 512 nm and weak red one at 620 nm under 365 nm excitation, were synthesized hydrothermally from alizarin carmine and citric acid. CDs@Al3+, with distinct aggregation-induced red-emssion enhancement and green-emssion quenchment, were prepared by adding Al3+ to the CDs solution. Inspiringly, due to intrinsic ratiometric FL variation (I620/I512), CDs@Al3+ engender a successive FL colour variation from red to green in response to different concentrations of F− with low limit of detection of 7.998 μM and wide linear range of 150–1200 µM based on excellent linearity correlation between R/G value and F− concentration. Furthermore, F− content in tap water, toothpaste and milk could be intelligently, speedily, and straightforwardly analyzed through the AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform. It is fervently desired that our study will motivate a brand-new perspective for the promotion of efficacious detection strategy and the extension of practical application promise.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artificial intelligence
YOLO v3 algorithm
Smartphone-based sensing
Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emssion
F−

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FL assay of Al3+ and F−
Construction and F− detection of AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform
AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld determination of F− in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A − C) CDs’ TEM images at various magnifications. (Inset of Fig. 1A is CDs’ HRTEM image. Inset of Fig. 1C is CDs’ diameter distribution). (D) CDs’ AFM topographic image. (Inset is the distribution of height profile along the white line). (E) CDs’ AFM 3D image. (F) CDs’ height distribution.
(A) XPS full spectrum of CDs. (B − D) XPS spectra of O1s, C1s and S2p for CDs. (E) FTIR spectrum of CDs.

(A) Ratiometric FL spectra and colour change of CDs solution at diverse Al3+ concentrations (0–400 μM) under 365 nm excitation, (B) corresponding linear relationship between I620/I512 and Al3+ concentrations, and (C) calculated CIE chromaticity diagram for Al3+ asssay. (D) Ratiometric FL spectra and colour change of CDs@Al3+ solution at diverse F− concentrations (0–1200 μM) under 365 nm excitation, (E) corresponding linear relationship between I620/I512 and F− concentrations, and (F) calculated CIE chromaticity diagram for F− assay.
Possible mechanisms of CDs in response to Al3+ and F−.
Diagram of YOLO v3 AI algorithm.

The usage of “Artificial Intelligent Assay” to analyze the concentration of F−. (Red arrow represents the fitting steps of F−, green arrow represents prediction steps of F−). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform of F− based on Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emssion enhanced CDs.
AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld determination of F− in tap water, toothpaste, and milk.

head.ce:title: Artificial intelligence-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection of fluoride ion by Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emssion enhanced carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Computer and Information Technology, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Computer and Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Computer and Information Technology, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 Schematic diagram of AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform of F− based on Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emssion enhanced CDs.

Artificial intelligence (AI)-integrated smartphone-based handheld determination platform, based on 3D printed accessory, Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emssion enhanced carbon dots (CDs) test strip, and smartphone with self-developed YOLO v3 AI algorithm-based application, proves the feasibility for intelligent real-time on-site quantitation of F− through tracking a consecutive fluorescence (FL) colour change. CDs, manifesting dual emission of moderate green emission at 512 nm and weak red one at 620 nm under 365 nm excitation, were synthesized hydrothermally from alizarin carmine and citric acid. CDs@Al3+, with distinct aggregation-induced red-emssion enhancement and green-emssion quenchment, were prepared by adding Al3+ to the CDs solution. Inspiringly, due to intrinsic ratiometric FL variation (I620/I512), CDs@Al3+ engender a successive FL colour variation from red to green in response to different concentrations of F− with low limit of detection of 7.998 μM and wide linear range of 150–1200 µM based on excellent linearity correlation between R/G value and F− concentration. Furthermore, F− content in tap water, toothpaste and milk could be intelligently, speedily, and straightforwardly analyzed through the AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform. It is fervently desired that our study will motivate a brand-new perspective for the promotion of efficacious detection strategy and the extension of practical application promise.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the ceaseless development of data processing capabilities and hardware devices of smartphones in recent years, smartphones as testing instruments have come on stage in a variety of domains including environmental governance [1,2], clinical diagnosis [3], as well as food/medicine quality management [4]. Very recently, smartphone-based handheld detection platform, integrating smartphone, 3D-printed accessory, and ratiometric fluorescence (FL) test strip, has enabled in-situ real-time visual identification [5], on which the test strip makes the visualization realize [6], smartphone endows with quantitative determination ability via calculating red–green–blue (RGB) and hue-saturation-value (HSV) values of the test strip [7], and 3D-printing affords a portable enclosed chamber with built-in UV light and paper strip slot [8]. In our recent work, utilizing the smartphone-based handheld detection platform, Shi et al. determined Mn(VII) and GSH and Chang et al. detected Hg2+ based on ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) [9,10]. Adopting the platform, Jiang et al. mixed CDs with AuNCs for identifying GSH [11] as well as glyphosate [12] and developed red-emitting CdTe quantum dots@silica nanoparticles@blue-emitting CDs probe for identifying thiram [13]. Wang et al. constructed a lanthanide-based ratiometric emission probe for on-site determination of tetracycline via the platform [14]. Yu et al. synthesized a Eu-MOFs-based ratiometric FL probe for on-site quantitation of H2O2 through the platform [15]. You et al. fabricated a dual-colour upconversion nanoprobe for onsite quantitation analysis of suppression of tumorigenicity 2 and brain natriuretic peptide and via the platform [16]. Nonetheless, the smartphone-based handheld detection platform requires manual data processing, which is time-consuming, labour-consuming, and easily interfered due to the uncertainty and subjectivity of human treatment.

Artificial intelligence (AI) has intrigued many academics since the concept was proposed, which can be defined as a computer algorithm that learns from data on its own convolutional neural networks and needn’t human intervention in the process [17,18]. For the smartphone-based handheld detection platform, the introduction of AI can generate and analyze data in large databases through algorithms, allowing intelligent speedy convenient portable detection and facilitating real-time on-site application [19,20]. Currently, several researches have reported AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform for visual monitoring. Adopting YOLO v3 AI algorithm-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform, Lu et al. have achieved intelligent detection of different analytes including Hg2+, Cu2+, sulfide, thiram, GSH, azodicarbonamide, and tetracycline antibiotics via polychromatic ratiometric FL sensors [21–25]. Nevertheless, the study on AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform is in its infancy and thus still is a vibrant topic of investigation.

Fluoride (F−), as a crucial trace element in biological systems, plays a pivotal part in osteoporosis treatment and dental caries prevention [26,27]. Notwithstanding, massive ingestion of F− could result in multiple diseases, including skeletal/dental fluorosis [28], DNA damage [29], and acute gastric and kidney illness [30]. Thus, it is of particular significance to exploit credible methods for accurate assay of F−. Nowadays, multiple analytical methods have been utilized to quantify F− including ion chromatography [31], 19F NMR [32], chemiluminescence [33], fluorescent spectroscopy [34], colorimetry [35], and ion-selective electrode [36]. Among these methods, fluorescent method has gained substantial attention due to favorable sensitivity and simple operation, of which ratiometric fluorescent method becomes superior alternatives to intensity-based one in recent years [37]. Zhang et al. confined green-emission eosin Y into the cavities of blue-emission UiO-66-NH2 to construct ratiometric FL F− sensor [38]. Wu et al. synthesized a ratiometric FL probe based on benzimidazole-naphthalene conjugate molecule to ratiometric recognize F− [39]. Inspired by that ratiometric FL sensor presents a rich colour variation range accompanying with intrinsic ratiometric FL variation [37], smartphone with built-in colour recognization application (APP) can be combined with ratiometric FL sensor for visual assay. Liu et al. synthesized a ratiometric FL nanoprobe, based on the mixtion of red FL ruthenium bipyridine molecule and Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced green-emssion enhanced CDs, for visual determination of F− by the smartphone platform [40]. Furthermore, integrating the smartphone platform and 3D-printed accessory, smartphone-based handheld detection platform has been constructed, which not only provide a consistent detection environment but also realize portable detection. Zhang et al. employed dual-emission quantum dots hybrid for portable detection of F− by the smartphone-based handheld detection platform [41]. Nonetheless, AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection of F− has not been reported so far.

Herein, adopting AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe, Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emssion enhanced CDs (CDs@Al3+), has been utilized to intelligently, speedily, straightforwardly, and conveniently quantify F− (Scheme 1 ). CDs, with dual emission of moderate green emission and weak red emission, were fabricated with alizarin carmine and citric acid through hydrothermal method, and CDs@Al3+, with distinct aggregation-induced red-emssion enhancement and green-emssion quenchment, were constructed by the introduction of Al3+ to the CDs solution. Intriguingly, in response to increasing F− concentration, CDs@Al3+ present a gradual decrease of red emission and a ratio rise of emerging green band accompanying with a continuous FL colour diversification from red to green, which can be devised as ratiometric FL paper sensor for visual recognition of F− based on good linear relationship between R/G value and F− concentration. Furthermore, AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform, combining 3D printed accessory, CDs@Al3+ test strip, and smartphone with self-developed YOLO v3 AI algorithm-based APP, could intelligently quantitatively analyse F− in tap water, toothpaste and milk. The proposed protocol offers an efficient detection platform in the analysis of real samples and manifest ground-breaking application promise in food safety.

Alizarin carmine (0.05 g) and citric acid (0.50 g) as precursors were first dissolved in 30 mL DDI water. Then, the homogeneous mixture was poured into 50 mL autoclave that was heated for 7 h at 200 °C. Later, the reaction was completed and the reactor was naturally cooled down to ambient temperature. Obtained crude product was centrifuged for 20 min at 3000 rpm. Subsequently, obtained supernatant was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed with 500–1000 Da dialysis membrane for 12 h. Ultimately, CDs solution was collected and deposited at 4 °C for further exploration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL assay of Al3+ and F−
ce:section.ce:para: For Al3+ detection, 80 μL CDs solution (2.1 g/L) was dispersed into 300 μL Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.0) with a final concentration of 0.4 g/L, and then various concentrations of Al3+ solution were separately added for 60 s. FL spectra were documented at 365 nm excitation.
Similarly, for F− detection, the different amount of F− was respectively added to CDs@Al3+ solution (0.4 g/L CDs, 400 μM Al3+) for 60 s. FL spectra were documented at 365 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and F− detection of AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform is composed of three components, namely, 3D-printed optical attachment, ratiometric FL test strip, and smartphone with self-developed APP based on YOLO v3 AI algorithm. The design specifications and usage instructions of the 3D printed accessory have been reported in our study [9]. The self-developed APP “Artificial Intelligent Assay” is deployed onto WeChat of smartphone, which mainly includes four components: automatical recognizing the position of the target and cutting it, reading out RGB and HSV values of the cut pictures, selecting the optimal correlation between the response signals and the target concentration for linear fitting, and direct predicting the target concentration in real samples. The required APP was self-designed by our group. The required sample library extraction code follows the link: https://github.com/zzwna/Artificial-Intelligence-Assay.
Unknown concentrations of F− in tap water, toothpaste, and milk can be directly read out through the AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform.

TEM images (Fig. 1 A − C) depict that CDs possess fantastic dispersion and quasi-spherical morphology. As shown in HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1A), CDs exhibit well-resolved crystalline structure with 0.21 nm interlayer lattice fringe spacing, having a total agreement with (101) face of graphite [42]. Inset of Fig. 1C presents that the size distribution of CDs ranges from 1.75 to 3.55 nm with average diameter of 2.65 ± 0.50 nm. XRD pattern exhibits a broad peak at 21.28°–38.98° (Figure S1), which is assigned to partial order in the interior of CDs resulting from covalently crosslinked frameworks [43]. AFM images (Fig. 1D − E) display exceptional dispersion of CDs. The height of CDs is from 2.80 to 4.50 nm and mean statistical height is estimated as 3.93 nm (Fig. 1F). Good consistency is acquired with TEM results.

Full spectrum of XPS of CDs (Fig. 2 A) exhibits three apparent bands at 527.84 eV, 281.06 eV, and 161.94 eV that correspond to O1s, C1s, and S2p, respectively. O1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2B) is decomposed to three peaks, namely, CO (531.9 eV), C − OH (532.7 eV), and SO (536.8 eV). For C1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2C), three bands at 289.1 eV, 286.7 eV, and 284.8 eV illustrate the presence of OC − O, C O, and CC. CDs’ S2p spectrum (Fig. 2D) is fitted into two peaks at 168.1 eV and 169.2 eV, attributing to the presence of S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively. As seen in FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2E), the peaks centered at 3176 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1 are attributed to O H and C H stretching vibration, respectively. The broadband at 2594 cm−1 corresponds to S H bond. The sharp enhanced bands at 1707 cm−1, 1627 cm−1, 1393 cm−1, and 1039 cm−1 are due to CO, CC, C O, and C C stretching vibration, respectively. The broadband at 660 cm−1 reveals the existence of C S bond. Coincident findings with XPS are gained. According to XPS and FTIR results, as-synthesized CDs’ surface might be surrounded by hydroxyl, carboxyl, as well as sulfur-containing groups.

UV–vis absorption (Fig. S2A) displays apparently two absorption peaks located at 330 nm and 494 nm, corresponding to π − π* transition of CC in CDs core and n − π* transition of C S/CO on CDs shell, respectively [44,45]. Excitation-emission spectra (Fig. S2A) manifest intriguing green and red dual-emission behavior with increasing excitation wavelength from 365 to 400 nm. Additionally, CDs’ solution is yellow under daylight whereas it emits green FL under 365 nm irradiation (inset of Fig. S2A). CDs’ FL quantum yield is 7.2% with rhodamine 6G as a reference. CDs’ mean FL lifetime of is 5.8305 ns (Fig. S2B).

To evaluate their feasibility in practical application, stability of CDs was explored. Fig. S3A − C present that FL intensity of CDs remains nearly constant under different concentrations of NaCl (0–4 M), 150 min exposure to xenon arc light, and 30 days storage, indicating that CDs possess outstanding stability. The superb tolerance for high ion strength, continuous irradiation, and long-term preservation endues CDs with practical application prospect.

Condition optimization mainly includes the following parameters: (a) pH value of the reaction system, (b) reaction temperature, (c) Al3+ reaction time, and (d) F− reaction time. As demonstrated in Fig. S4A − D, the optimal experimental conditions and test results are as follows: (a) the optimal pH value is 7.0, (b) the optimal reaction temperature is 20 °C, (c) The best reaction time of Al3+ is 60 s, (d) the best reaction time of F− is 60 s.

Typically, ratiometric identification renders FL probes improved precision and reliability via calculating intensity ratio of well-resolved FL bands. To evaluate CDs’ selectivity, the effect of various ions and amino acids on FL intensity was inspected under 365 nm excitation (Fig. S5A − C). Unexpectedly, FL intensity ratio (I620/I512) remarkably increases with rising Al3+ concentrations while other competitive substances show negligible effect, implying that CDs have high selectivity to Al3+ and can serve as an efficient Al3+ ratiometric FL sensor. Furthermore, other analytes at concentration up to 400 μM display trifling interference (Fig. S5A − C).

Detection ability of CDs for Al3+ was investigated in range of 0–400 μM. With increasing concentration of Al3+, the emission of CDs at 512 nm gradually decreases even disappears while that at 620 nm significantly enhances (Fig. 3 A), which concurrently exhibits continuous FL colour variation from green to red (inset of Fig. 3A). As illustrated in Fig. 3B, two excellent linear relationships are gained between FL intensity ratios (I620/I512) and Al3+ concentrations in the scope of 25–175 and 200–400 μM. A limit of detection (LOD) of 4.2 nM is calculated according to signal/noise ratio of 3 and far less than previous reports (Table S1), corroborating that the prepared Al3+ sensor possesses superior sensitivity. Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3C) presents consistent FL colour change with CDs solution exhibited in inset of Fig. 3A, verifying that visual recognition of CDs for Al3+ is of admirable feasibility.

Selectivity of CDs@Al3+ was appraised in the presence of different ions and amino acids. Accidentally, on introducing F− to CDs@Al3+, FL intensity ratio (I620/I512) evidently decreases whereas other analytes have little effect (Fig. S6A − C), indicating excellent selectivity of CDs@Al3+ for F−. Meanwhile, negligible interference of other competitive substances is presented at concentrations up to 1200 μM (Fig. S6A − C).

Sensing capability of CDs@Al3+ for F− was studied in the range of 0–1200 μM. When F− concentration increases, the emission at 620 nm apparently decreases whereas a ratio rising peak at 512 nm emerges (Fig. 3D), during which FL colour continuously returns from red to green (inset in Fig. 3D). Two exceptional linear relationships are exhibited between FL intensity ratio (I620/I512) and F− concentration in the scope of 75–825 μM and 900–1200 μM (Fig. 3E). A LOD of 15.9 nM demonstrates superior sensitivity of proposed probe for F− to reported probes (Table S2). This probe system has an ultralow LOD and a wider detection range for FL detection, which shows more excellent performance by comparing with others (Table S3). CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3F) accurately depicts FL colour of CDs@Al3+ under different F− concentrations, corresponding to FL colour shift trend of CDs@Al3+ solution presented in inset of Fig. 3D.

By virtue of smaller radius and more positive charges, Al3+ is considered to be hard acid. The surface of CDs is surrounded by carboxyl, hydroxyl, as well as sulfur-containing groups, of which O of carboxyl groups is considered to be hard base. As Al3+ is introduced to CDs solution, hard base O of carboxyl groups could have close affinity with Al3+ based on hard and soft acids and bases rule. To verify the proposed coupling of CDs with Al3+, zeta potentials and FTIR spectra were carried out. Al3+ renders zeta potential of CDs solution increase significantly from − 7.30 to − 1.11 mV (Figure S7), probably resulting from the consumption of carboxyl groups and confirming close affinity between CDs and Al3+. In contrast to FTIR spectrum of CDs, the emergence of three bands at 1354.78, 1008.91, and 876.50 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of CDs@Al3+ approves the development of Al − O bond (Figure S8). Simultaneously, Al3+ renders mean particle size of CDs increase from 2.65 to 9.09 nm (Figure S9A − B), and average height of CDs increase from 3.93 nm to 17.28 nm (Figure S10A − B), indicating that the interaction between Al3+ and CDs triggers CDs’ agglomeration (Fig. 4 ). For one thing, combining Al3+ with CDs quenches green FL of CDs by virtue of energy transfer. For another thing, Al3+ triggers aggregation-induced red-emssion enhancement of CDs by restricting free rotation of the functional groups on CDs surface, which inhibits nonradiative transition, promotes radiative transition, and enhances red FL [46].

F− is more electronegative than oxygen and thus fluorine is harder base than oxygen. Subsequent addition F− into CDs@Al3+ solution, F− can competitively take Al3+ away from CDs’ surface to form [AlF6]3− [47]. To validate the proposed mechanism, zeta potentials and FTIR spectra were implemented. F− makes zeta potential of CDs@Al3+ decrease significantly from –1.11 to –6.15 mV (Figure S7), resulting from the disjunction of Al3+ from CDs surface and verifying the formation of [AlF6]3−. Furthermore, comparing with FTIR spectrum of CDs@Al3+, the disappearance of three bands at 1354.78, 1008.91, and 876.50 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of CDs@Al3++F− confirms the breakage of Al − O bond (Figure S8). Meanwhile, F− renders mean particle size of CDs@Al3+ decline from 9.09 nm to 3.34 nm (Figure S9C − D) and average height of CDs@Al3+ decrease from 17.28 nm to 4.50 nm (Figure S10C − D), illustrating that F− induces the release of aggregation of CDs@Al3+ (Fig. 4). Therefore, subsequent adding F− to CDs@Al3+ solution not only prohibits energy transfer on the CDs shell, resulting in the restoration of green FL, but also recovers the rotation of the functional groups of CDs, enhancing nonradiative transition, inhibiting radiative transition, and causing red FL reduction.

YOLO v3 AI algorithm model is adopted in self-developed APP “Artificial Intelligent Assay”. The algorithm model is mainly composed of Darknet-53 backbone network structure and YOLO detection layers, in which the Darknet-53 network structure is used to extract feature information of test strip and the YOLO detection layers are utilized to predict category and position information of test strip. The model adds a paper strip category according to the voc2007 training set format with a total of 22 categories. Diagram of YOLO v3 AI algorithm for test strip extraction process is displayed in Fig. 5 . First, test strip image with a size of 416 × 416 is imported into the Darknet-53 feature extraction network. Next, through a series of convolution neural networks, three characteristic layers with sizes of 13 × 13, 26 × 26, and 52 × 52 are obtained that is called feature processing and extraction. Then, in order to acquire deeper feature expressions and fulfill more accurate outcomes, the three feature maps are fused by upsampling, downsampling, stacking, fusion, and convolution processing, and final three characteristic layers including 13 × 13 × 81, 26 × 26 × 81, and 52 × 52 × 81 are acquired, which accurately account for 1/32, 1/16, and 1/8 of the original image, respectively. Finally, export the thorough object information of the whole image and frame the object in the original image. The object detection process for an imported test strip image can be finished so far.

To obtain the optimal trained model, the parameters of the model including the amount of training times, batch size, and learning rate are adjusted according to the outcomes of the training and validation sets. Then, the test set images are used to appraise the capability of the YOLO v3 model for identifying and locating the object paper strip. The frequently-used evaluation indicators including precision, recall, and F1-score are displayed in Table S4, of which calculation formula are (1)−(3):
(1)
p r e c i s i o n =
T P
T P + F P
(2)
r e c a l l =
T P
T P + F N
(3)
F 1 =
2 × p r e c i s i o n × r e c a l l
p r e c i s i o n +
r e c a l l
wherein, TP corresponds to the number of images that are the paper strip object and are correctly recognized through the model. FP corresponds to the number of images that do not belong to the paper strip object but are incorrectly recognized via the model. FN corresponds to the number of image s that are the paper strip object but are not correctly recognized through the model. As displayed in Table S4, the precision reaches 94.8%, the recall rate measures up to 100.0% and F1 reaches 97.3%, illustrating that the proposed YOLO v3 AI model may recognize and locate the target paper strip with high accuracy.

The usage of “Artificial Intelligent Assay” is presented in Fig. 6 . To build a standard curve (direction indicated by red arrow), the picture acquisition methods have real-time shooting and image uploading. The image-uploading mode is selected in the research. Upon importing into the APP, the images are automatically identified, and the FL is documented through the YOLO v3 algorithm. The analysis results of APP show RGB and HSV values of each test strip images. On this basis, as shown in Figure S11, the relationships are constructed between F− concentration (0–1200 μM) and various signal parameters (R/G, R/B, G/B, (G + R)/B, G, and R). Among them, as shown in Figure S12, the linear correlation between R/G and F− concentration is the best with wide linear range of 150–1200 μM and a low LOD of 7.998 μM, which exhibits a good agreement with that measured by fluorescent spectrophotometry. Thus, “Artificial Intelligent Assay” APP can quickly accurately quantify F− concentration via the R/G value.
When unknown concentration of F− is detected, upload test strip image, choose the optimal linearity correlation, and then output concentration of F− (direction indicated by green arrow).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld determination of F− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of CDs@Al3+-based test strip for F− was appraised by dropping different solutions of anions on test strips. F− makes FL colour of CDs@Al3+-based paper strips vary from red to green but other anions exhibit trifling effect (Figure S13), suggesting that CDs@Al3+-based test strips can be employed for specific differentiation of F−.

To validate the practicality of AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld detection platform, standard recovery assays were implemented in tap water, toothpaste, and milk. Analysis results of F− in different actual samples using the AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld sensing system are revealed in Table 1 . The recoveries of F− in tap water, toothpaste, and milk are calculated to be 99.98%−101.65%, 100.54%−101.66%, and 99.24%−99.83%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are less than 4.58%. Such an outcome corroborates the feasibility of proposed method for F− detection in food safety domain.

CDs, with modest green emitting and weak red one, have been fabricated through hydrothermal treatment of alizarin carmine and citric acid. As Al3+ is introduced to the CDs solution, the CDs@Al3+ system displays aggregation-induced red-emission enhancement and green-emission quenchment. With increasing F− concentration, CDs@Al3+ manifests intrinsic ratiometric FL variation (I620/I512) accompanying a consecutive FL colour transformation from red to green, which lays a foundation for wide-spectrum visual detection of F−. AI-integrated smartphone-based handheld determination platform, integrating 3D printed accessory, CDs@Al3+ based paper sensor, and smartphone with self-developed APP based on YOLO v3 AI algorithm is first employed for intelligent, speedy, straightforward, and convenient identification of F− in tap water, toothpaste, and milk. The developed sensing methodologies allow instrument-free non-skilled-personnel screening of food safety, which will become superior alternatives to traditional sensing technology.

Liru Yan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Bianxiang Zhang: Validation. Zhiwei Zong: Software. Wei Zhou: Validation. Shaomin Shuang: Funding acquisition, Validation. Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.07.125.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric fluorescent paper strip with smartphone platform for quantitative detection of cadmium ions in real samples
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Q. Wang, L.G. Da, L. Yang, S.Y. Chu, F. Yang, S.M. Yu, C.L. Jiang, Colorimetric fluorescent paper strip with smartphone platform for quantitative detection of cadmium ions in real samples, J. Hazard. Mater. 392 (2020) 122506−122513.
